1.synthesis: Now concerning food offered to idols: 'We all possess knowledge.' But knowledge puffs up while love builds up (1 Corinthians 8:1). If anyone imagines he knows something, he does not yet know as he ought to know. But if anyone loves God, he is known by God (1 Corinthians 8:2–3). Therefore, as to eating food offered to idols: 'An idol has no real existence and there is no God but one' (1 Corinthians 8:4). Although there may be so-called gods in heaven or on earth—as indeed there are many such 'gods' and many 'lords'—yet for them there is one God, the Father, from whom all things come and for whom they exist, and one Lord, Jesus Christ, through whom all things came and through whom they exist (1 Corinthians 8:5–6).
1.historical_context: Food offered to idols (eidolothyta) was a pervasive reality in Corinthian social life: meat was routinely sacrificed at temples before being sold in the marketplace or served at social dinners (many of which were held in temple precincts). The 'strong' Corinthians who 'possessed knowledge' argued—correctly in principle—that idols are nothing real, so the meat is not spiritually contaminated. Paul agrees with the theology (v.4–6) while rejecting the behavior it licenses when it harms others. The 'one God, one Lord' confession (vv.5–6) is one of the earliest Christian expansions of Jewish monotheism to incorporate Jesus alongside the Father—a binitarian reformulation of the Shema (Deuteronomy 6:4: 'The LORD our God, the LORD is one'). All things are 'through' Christ and 'for' the Father—a christological cosmology that positions Jesus as the mediator of creation.
1.literary_structure: The knowledge/love contrast (vv.1–3) frames the entire idol-food discussion: the strong are right in their knowledge but wrong in their application because they have privileged knowledge over love. Paul's agreement with the strong's monotheistic theology (vv.4–6) and his christological expansion of it both affirm the theological premise and intensify the responsibility: those who know the one God and one Lord are precisely those who should know that love for the weaker brother takes priority over the exercise of theological insight. The argument continues in 8:7–13 with the application to those whose conscience is not yet free of idol-associations.
1.theological_application: 'Knowledge puffs up, but love builds up' (v.1) is the controlling principle of the entire idol-food discussion and one of the most important hermeneutical principles in Paul. Correct theological knowledge is not sufficient to determine ethical action; the love-consideration (who is built up or harmed by this action) must be integrated into every application of theological truth. The church continually faces situations where the theologically informed know they are free from certain restrictions while others in the community have not yet reached that freedom. The danger of privileged theological knowledge is that it can become a tool of social distinction within the community—the 'strong' versus the 'weak'—rather than a resource for communal upbuilding. The monotheism of verses 4–6 is not merely doctrinal affirmation but ethical ground: if there is only one God and one Lord, then the meat's origin in idol-worship is theologically meaningless—but the brother's conscience is not meaningless, because he too belongs to the one God and one Lord who gave himself for him.
1.christology: The christological expansion of the Shema in verse 6 is one of Paul's most remarkable theological statements. The Shema's 'one LORD' is divided between 'one God, the Father' and 'one Lord, Jesus Christ'—a binitarian formulation that incorporates Jesus into the divine identity without abandoning monotheism. The Father is the source ('from whom are all things') and goal ('for whom we exist'); Jesus Christ is the agent ('through whom are all things') and the sphere of existence ('through whom we exist'). This makes Jesus the mediator of both creation and new creation—the one through whom God's creative and redemptive purposes flow. The 'all things through him' echoes John 1:3 and Colossians 1:16, positioning this christological confession as the theological ground for the community's ethical discernment about idol-food.

7.pericope_label: Do Not Cause a Brother to Fall
7.synthesis: Not everyone possesses this knowledge. Some, through former association with idols, eat food as genuinely offered to an idol, and their conscience—weak and defiled—is harmed (1 Corinthians 8:7). Food will not commend them to God—they are no worse or better for eating or not eating (1 Corinthians 8:8). But take care that this right of yours does not become a stumbling block to the weak (1 Corinthians 8:9). For if anyone sees someone with knowledge reclining at table in an idol's temple, will not the conscience of him who is weak be emboldened to eat food offered to idols—and so his conscience, if it is weak, is defiled? (1 Corinthians 8:10–11.) And by thus sinning against your brothers and wounding their conscience when it is weak, you sin against Christ (1 Corinthians 8:12). Therefore, if food makes his brother stumble, he will never eat meat again, so as not to cause his brother to fall (1 Corinthians 8:13).
7.historical_context: The 'weak' person Paul describes has a different experiential history with the idol-food: for them, eating it still carries the weight of their former religious life in the idol-cults. When they see a 'knowledgeable' believer eating at an idol-temple table, they may be emboldened to eat—not in freedom but in imitation—and in doing so violate their own conscience. The problem is not the eating but the conscience-violation: doing something while believing it is wrong is sinful regardless of whether the thing itself is neutral. The 'sinning against Christ' escalation (v.12) is the strongest possible framing: wounding the conscience of a weaker brother is not merely interpersonal rudeness but an offense against the Lord who died for that brother.
7.literary_structure: The argument moves from the weak person's situation (v.7) to the neutrality-of-food principle (v.8) to the warning about exercising rights (v.9) to the concrete stumbling scenario (vv.10–11) to the christological escalation (v.12) to the personal vow (v.13). The vow—'I will never eat meat again'—is hyperbolic in scope but absolute in principle: Paul is willing to give up a legitimate pleasure permanently rather than risk causing a brother to stumble. This models the love-over-knowledge priority of v.1 in practice.
7.theological_application: The 'destroying the one for whom Christ died' principle (implied in v.11) appears also in Romans 14:15 and is one of the most powerful ethical arguments in Paul: Christ's death establishes the value of every community member, including the most theologically naive. No exercise of legitimate Christian freedom is worth destroying that person by emboldening them to sin against their conscience. The church must take seriously that the 'rights' of the mature are always exercised in community, and community relations constrain freedom at the point of harm to others. The personal vow of verse 13 is the practical conclusion: genuine love for brothers and sisters produces willingness to limit one's own freedom permanently for their sake. This is not legalism but the ethics of cruciform community.
7.christology: 'Sinning against your brothers and wounding their conscience—you sin against Christ' (v.12) identifies the risen Lord so intimately with the members of his body that injury to them is injury to him. This is the same principle as Matthew 25:40 ('whatever you did for the least of these my brothers you did for me') applied to harm rather than care: the one who wounds a member of Christ's body wounds Christ himself. The brother for whom the strong person's cavalier freedom is destructive is the brother for whom Christ died—a cost that establishes his infinite worth and makes any harm to him a serious christological matter. Freedom that comes at the cost of harm to the one Christ died for is a freedom the cruciform community cannot afford to exercise.
